title: NOC 92015 – Supervisors, textile, fabric, fur and leather products processing and manufacturing | GTR Immigration
description: Supervisors under NOC 92015 are the operational leaders of Canada's specialized garment, textile, and tannery sectors. As a TEER 2 occupation, these professionals manage the production of everything from industrial technical textiles used in aerospace to high-end fashion and automotive leather interiors. In 2026, as Canada pivots toward "Circular Textiles" and sustainable fabric manufacturing, these supervisors are essential for managing high-tech recycling and eco-friendly dyeing processes. For immigration, this role is a strong candidate for the Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP) and the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Level2Major Group92 – Processing, manufacturing and utilities supervisorsSub-Major Group920 – Processing, manufacturing and utilities supervisorsMinor Group9201 – Supervisors, processing and manufacturing occupationsNOC Code92015

duties: Supervise, coordinate, and schedule the activities of workers who operate machines to spin, weave, knit, dye, or finish textiles and leather.
Establish work schedules and procedures and coordinate activities with other production departments to meet delivery deadlines.
Resolve work problems and recommend measures to improve productivity and the quality of fabrics and leather products.
Requisition materials and supplies and manage inventory for specialized fibers or tanning chemicals.
Train workers in job duties, safety procedures, and the operation of new computer-controlled (CNC) textile machinery.
Ensure strict adherence to quality control standards and environmental regulations regarding chemical disposal.
Prepare detailed production, safety, and personnel reports for senior plant management.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is typically required.
Work Experience: Several years of experience in the specific textile, fabric, or leather processing occupation supervised are mandatory.
Education: A college diploma in textile technology or a related manufacturing discipline is increasingly preferred for modern automated facilities.

Certifications:Internal company certifications for specific machinery (e.g., Shima Seiki or Stoll knitting machines).WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) for handling dyes and tanning chemicals.
Internal company certifications for specific machinery (e.g., Shima Seiki or Stoll knitting machines).
WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) for handling dyes and tanning chemicals.
Strong leadership, color-matching vision, and proficiency in digital production management systems (PLM) are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: Supervisors in specialized "Technical Textiles" (e.g., bulletproof vests or aerospace composites) often earn 15–20% above the median wage.
job_outlook: The outlook is Stable. While general garment manufacturing has shifted overseas, Canada’s focus on Advanced Technical Textiles and Sustainable Fashion is driving a resurgence in local processing. Projections indicate a consistent need for supervisors to replace retiring plant managers in the Montreal and Toronto garment districts.
labour_market_demand: The demand for textile and fabric processing supervisors is Good, with specific "Hot Zones" in Canada's traditional manufacturing hubs. In 2026, Quebec (Montreal) and Ontario (Toronto/Kitchener) are reporting a steady need for supervisors to manage the production of protective technical clothing and automotive upholstery. As the industry moves toward "On-Demand Manufacturing," there is a specialized demand for supervisors who can manage small-batch, high-tech production lines using digital knitting and automated cutting technology.
Technical Textile Leadership: Lead the production of advanced materials used in Canada’s thriving aerospace and medical device sectors.
Sustainability Innovation: Manage the implementation of closed-loop textile recycling and low-water-use fabric treatment technologies.
Artisanal and Industrial Mix: Work in a sector that balances traditional leather/fur craftsmanship with state-of-the-art automated manufacturing.
Strategic PR Pathways: This TEER 2 role is a high-priority "Skilled Worker" category for several provincial streams and Express Entry Category-Based Selection.
pathways: Express Entry (Category-Based Selection): Potential targeting under "Trade Occupations" or specific manufacturing categories.
OINP Employer Job Offer (Foreign Worker Stream): A direct route for those with a job offer from an Ontario-based textile or leather manufacturer.
Quebec Skilled Worker Program (RSWP): A high-priority pathway for supervisors in Montreal’s extensive fashion and textile sector.
New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A temporary measure targeting 33,000 workers already in Canada, including those in essential manufacturing sectors.
